title: Top Algorithm Patterns for Coding Interviews
description: Pattern recognition guide for coding interviews. Learn to identify which pattern applies to a problem, not memorize solutions. Each pattern includes identifying signals, common problems, and the core technique.

patterns.name: Two Pointers
signals: Finding pairs with a target sum
patterns.technique: Use two pointers moving toward each other (or in the same direction) to reduce O(n^2) to O(n). Start/end pointers for sorted arrays, slow/fast for linked lists.

patterns.name: Sliding Window
patterns.technique: Maintain a window [left, right] over the array. Expand right to include elements, shrink left when constraint is violated. Track window state with a hash map or counter.
examples: Maximum Sum Subarray of Size K

patterns.name: Binary Search
patterns.technique: Search on the answer space, not just the input. If you can define a condition that's false then true (or vice versa), binary search applies. Use left + (right - left) / 2 to avoid overflow.

patterns.name: BFS / Level-Order Traversal
patterns.technique: Queue-based traversal processing nodes level by level. Track visited nodes. For multi-source BFS, initialize queue with all sources. Level tracking with queue size snapshot.

patterns.name: DFS / Backtracking
patterns.technique: Recursively explore choices, backtrack when constraint is violated. Use a visited set for graph DFS. Prune early to improve performance. Pass state by reference and undo after recursion.

patterns.name: Dynamic Programming
signals: Optimal substructure (optimal solution uses optimal sub-solutions)
patterns.technique: Define state clearly, write recurrence relation, identify base cases. Decide top-down (memoization) or bottom-up (tabulation). Optimize space if only previous row/state needed.

patterns.name: Greedy
signals: Local optimal leads to global optimal
patterns.technique: Sort by a key metric, then make the locally optimal choice at each step. Prove correctness by exchange argument or showing optimal substructure. Often combined with sorting.

patterns.name: Monotonic Stack
patterns.technique: Maintain a stack where elements are in monotonically increasing (or decreasing) order. When a new element violates the monotonic property, pop and process. The popped element's answer involves the new element.

patterns.name: Union-Find (Disjoint Set)
patterns.technique: Track parent of each element. Find: follow parent pointers to root (with path compression). Union: merge two sets by connecting roots (with rank/size). Nearly O(1) amortized operations.

patterns.name: Topological Sort
patterns.technique: Kahn's algorithm: compute in-degree for each node, start with in-degree 0 nodes in queue, process and decrement neighbors' in-degree. Alternatively: DFS with post-order recording. Cycle detection: if processed count < total nodes.

patterns.name: Trie (Prefix Tree)
patterns.technique: Tree where each node represents a character. Insert by traversing/creating nodes for each character. Search by traversal. Store isEndOfWord flag at terminal nodes. Space: O(alphabet_size * average_word_length * num_words).

patterns.name: Heap / Priority Queue
patterns.technique: Use min-heap for K largest (evict smallest), max-heap for K smallest (evict largest). Two heaps for running median. Priority queue for merge operations. Time: O(log K) per operation.

patterns.name: Graph: Dijkstra / Shortest Path
patterns.technique: Priority queue with (distance, node). Process closest unvisited node, relax edges to neighbors. For negative weights, use Bellman-Ford. For all-pairs, use Floyd-Warshall. Time: O((V+E) log V) with binary heap.

patterns.name: Bit Manipulation
patterns.technique: XOR: a^a=0, a^0=a (find single number). AND with (n-1): clear lowest set bit (power of 2 check, count bits). Bitmask: represent subsets as integers, iterate with i & (1<<j).

patterns.name: Interval Merging / Sweep Line
patterns.technique: Sort intervals by start time. Merge overlapping intervals by comparing current end with next start. For point-in-time queries, use sweep line: convert intervals to +1 (start) and -1 (end) events, sort, and scan.
